one_line_takeaway: LoRA fails to forget weight-poisoning backdoors not because of low rank but because its updates have insufficient spectral strength and residual alignment with trigger-sensitive pretrained subspaces; RoRA's combination of clean-strengthened dropout regularization, trigger-orthogonality penalty, and post-training layerwise spectral rescaling closes this gap, cutting ASR by 70-95% relative to LoRA across BERT/RoBERTa/LLaMA with near full-fine-tuning clean accuracy.
key_finding: Across all 6 model-dataset pairs (BERT/RoBERTa/LLaMA x SST-2/CR/CoLA) and both attack types (BadNet, InSent), standard LoRA leaves the model highly vulnerable: LoRA typically attains ASR near 100% and negative Delta (e.g., BERT-BadNet-SST-2: LoRA CA 92.00 / ASR 99.70 / Delta -7.70). LoRA variants (PiSSA, DoRA, OLoRA) show only limited, inconsistent improvement. RoRA achieves the strongest and most stable robustness, typically reducing ASR by 70-95% relative to LoRA while keeping clean accuracy within about 0.5-2.0 points of FFT/LoRA: e.g., BERT-BadNet-SST-2 ASR drops from 99.70 (LoRA) to 7.48 (RoRA), Delta improves from -7.70 to 84.98; LLaMA-BadNet-SST-2 Delta rises from -5.62 (LoRA) and 56.95 (FFT) to 91.07 (RoRA); RoBERTa-InSent-CR Delta improves from 24.65 (Back-Translation) to 88.12 (RoRA). Ablation (Table 4) shows each of the three RoRA components (clean-strengthened regularization Cl, trigger-insensitive regularization Tr, post-training spectral rescaling Pt) contributes complementarily -- Tr alone can leave ASR very high under InSent (93-98%) while Cl alone already cuts ASR below 25% in several settings, and the full combination gives the lowest ASR/highest Delta in every case. Efficiency: RoRA adds modest per-iteration training-time overhead over LoRA (+6ms BERT, +15ms RoBERTa, +6ms LLaMA on SST-2/BadNet, <=17% relative slowdown, Table 3).
core_contribution: The paper explains, both theoretically and empirically, why standard LoRA fine-tuning fails to remove weight-poisoning backdoors from a pretrained language model even after clean-data fine-tuning, attributing the failure to spectral properties of LoRA updates rather than to low rank per se. It proves a 'critical scaling threshold' (Proposition 4.2) beyond which a LoRA update's spectral strength and alignment with clean-task directions can force a triggered input's logit margin to favor the clean label over the backdoor target. Using SVD analysis of trained LoRA adapters (BERT, RoBERTa, LLaMA), the authors show LoRA updates have max singular values far below those of the pretrained weights (spectral ratio 10^0-10^2 across layers, Fig. 1) and retain small but nonzero cosine alignment (roughly 0.04-0.16, Fig. 2) with pretrained/trigger-relevant singular directions -- explaining empirically observed forgetting failure. Building on this diagnosis, the paper introduces RoRA (Regularized Low-Rank Adaptation), which adds (1) clean-strengthened regularization (dropout on pretrained weights during fine-tuning to force ∆W to encode more clean-task signal), (2) trigger-insensitive regularization (a soft orthogonality penalty Ω(A,B)=||U^T B||_F^2 + ||AV||_F^2 between the LoRA factors and the pretrained weight's singular subspaces), and (3) post-training spectral rescaling (setting s = σmax(Wpre)/σmax(ΔW) per-layer on the top three classification-relevant layers, without needing a triggered validation set).
threat_model: Weight-poisoning backdoor attack on a pretrained language model: the model is poisoned during pretraining/upstream training so that inputs containing a specific trigger are mapped to an adversary-chosen target label. The attacker has partial knowledge of the downstream task (label distribution) but no access to the actual downstream training data, so poisoning is done via a proxy dataset with similar label distribution (e.g., IMDB used to poison a model later fine-tuned on SST-2). The defender's goal is to run standard downstream fine-tuning (via LoRA) on clean data to overwrite/forget the embedded backdoor while preserving clean-task accuracy; the defense does not assume access to triggered validation samples or explicit trigger detection.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: evaluation is against standard/representative backdoor attacks and defenses only, with comparison to more recent adaptive attacks/defenses left for future work; experiments are on moderate-scale models (BERT-large, RoBERTa-large, LLaMA), with full verification on very large models (GPT-3/PaLM-2/GPT-4 scale) left open; the theoretical analysis (Proposition 4.2) is linearized/spectral and derived for a single linear classifier layer, so extending it to fully nonlinear, multi-layer transformer dynamics is left as future work. Additionally (not stated as a limitation by the authors but evident from the results table), RoRA's Delta on CoLA remains markedly lower than on SST-2/CR in several settings (e.g., BERT-BadNet-CoLA Delta only 78.47 vs 84.98 on SST-2, and RoBERTa-InSent-CoLA ASR remains 74.90% despite RoRA), and post-training spectral rescaling is applied only to the top three layers by design choice rather than validated as globally optimal.

datasets_used: IMDB (Maas et al., 2011, used as proxy poisoning dataset for SST-2)
MR (Pang and Lee, 2005, used as proxy poisoning dataset for CR)
SST-2 used as proxy poisoning dataset for CoLA
quantitative_results: BERT-BadNet-SST-2: LoRA CA 92.00 / ASR 99.70 / Delta -7.70 vs RoRA CA 92.46 / ASR 7.48 / Delta 84.98
BERT-BadNet-CR: LoRA CA 89.50 / ASR 92.58 / Delta -3.08 vs RoRA CA 88.65 / ASR 2.70 / Delta 85.95

LLaMA-BadNet-SST-2: LoRA Delta -5.62 (implied from ablation baseline row), FFT Delta 56.95 (main table LLaMA-BadNet-SST-2 FFT: CA 92.20/ASR 33.66/Delta 58.54, text cites 56.95), RoRA Delta 91.07 (main table RoRA: CA 93.74/ASR 2.31/Delta 91.43, text cites 91.07)
RoBERTa-InSent-CR: Back Translation Delta 24.65 (text) vs RoRA Delta 88.12 (main table RoRA: CA 92.90/ASR 4.78/Delta 88.12)
Spectral ratio sigma_max(Wpre)/sigma_max(delta_W) across layers spans roughly 10^0 to 10^2 for BERT/RoBERTa models (Figure 1)
Cosine similarity between leading LoRA singular vector and top-32 pretrained singular vectors ranges roughly 0.04-0.16 across layers (Figure 2)
Ablation RoBERTa-BadNet: baseline (no reg) CA 95.71/ASR 99.74/Delta -4.03; Cl-only CA 96.16/ASR 10.34/Delta 85.82; Tr-only CA 95.33/ASR 13.42/Delta 81.91; full RoRA CA 95.99/ASR 6.49/Delta 89.50
Ablation LLaMA-InSent: baseline CA 95.28/ASR 100.0/Delta -4.72; Cl-only CA 95.28/ASR 12.76/Delta 82.52; Tr-only CA 95.55/ASR 98.35/Delta -2.80; full RoRA CA 94.29/ASR 11.88/Delta 82.41
Per-iteration training time (ms) on SST-2/BadNet: LoRA/RoRA = BERT 94/100, RoBERTa 90/105, LLaMA 84/90 (Table 3), i.e. overhead <=17%
Dropout-rate sweep: p=0.05 for LLaMA gives ASR 54.13% (weak forgetting); p=0.30 gives CA 94% (RoBERTa) / 88.63% (LLaMA) with sharply increased ASR; best around p ~ 0.1
Regularization-strength sweep: lambda in {1,5,10,15,20}; RoBERTa ASR stays low for lambda 1-15 but rises to 16.5% at lambda=20; LLaMA ASR stays below 3.2% across all lambda values

Poisoned pretrained models: 1,500 clean-label poisoned samples with target label 0; ASR consistently exceeds 95% before downstream fine-tuning (satisfying Assumption A1); pretrained-only clean accuracy without fine-tuning (Table 1): LLaMA/RoBERTa/BERT on IMDB->SST-2 = 51.13/81.27/75.84%, on MR->CR = 75.10/83.87/86.19%
